unit.name: GAN practical fixes, theory, architectures, and limitations
unit.description: Covers non-saturating GAN training, theoretical optimum, major GAN architectures, latent interpolation, and practical limitations.
unit.summary: The instructor analyzes early GAN training dynamics: a randomly initialized generator outputs obvious noise, so the discriminator quickly learns to reject generated samples, placing the generator in a flat-gradient region of the original log(1 - D(G(z))) objective [ts=968s], [ts=1026s]. A practical non-saturating trick is introduced: train G to minimize -log D(G(z)) instead, which gives stronger generator gradients at the start of training [ts=1282s], [ts=1290s]. The theoretical justification is sketched: for any fixed pG, the inner discriminator optimum has D*_G(x)=pdata(x)/(pdata(x)+pG(x)), and the outer problem is minimized when pG=pdata, though finite-capacity networks and finite-data optimization give no convergence guarantee [ts=1341s], [ts=1392s]. Architecturally, the lecture highlights DC-GAN as the first non-toy GAN architecture and StyleGAN as a more modern design with adaptive normalization [ts=1471s], [ts=1519s]. GAN latent interpolation is shown as evidence that generator latent spaces can be smooth [ts=1538s], but the lecturer warns that mode collapse or memorizing a few training samples would not yield such smoothness [ts=1684s]. The segment closes by contrasting GANs with VAEs: GANs can produce crisp, high-quality images, but are unstable and hard to diagnose, whereas VAEs have explicit encoders but tend to produce blurry samples [ts=1600s], [ts=1804s].

key_points.text: At early training, a random generator emits obvious noise and the discriminator quickly solves real-vs-fake classification.
The original generator objective has nearly flat gradients when D(G(z)) is close to 0.
The common non-saturating trick trains G to minimize -log D(G(z)) for stronger early gradients.
For fixed pG, the optimal discriminator is D*_G(x)=pdata(x)/(pdata(x)+pG(x)).
The ideal outer objective is minimized when pG(x)=pdata(x), but finite networks and data give no convergence guarantee.
DC-GAN was highlighted as the first GAN architecture with non-trivial, non-toy image results.
StyleGAN uses a more complex architecture with adaptive normalization.
GAN latent vectors can be interpolated so generated images change smoothly, but collapse or memorization can break this.

kp_catalog.name: GAN instability diagnostics
kp_catalog.description: GAN value and loss curves are not reliable progress indicators because the game is non-stationary and jointly adversarial.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gAN instability diagnostics clarifies gAN value and loss curves are not reliable progress indicators because the game is non-stationary and jointly adversarial, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Non-saturating generator loss
kp_catalog.description: Replacing the generator's original objective with -log D(G(z)) gives stronger early gradients.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because non-saturating generator loss clarifies replacing the generator's original objective with -log D(G(z)) gives stronger early gradients, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: GAN optimal discriminator theory
kp_catalog.description: For fixed pG, the optimal discriminator is pdata/(pdata+pG), making pG=pdata the ideal solution under assumptions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gAN optimal discriminator theory clarifies for fixed pG, the optimal discriminator is pdata/(pdata+pG), making pG=pdata the ideal solution under assumptions, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: GAN architectures and latent interpolation
kp_catalog.description: DC-GAN, StyleGAN, and smooth latent interpolation illustrate practical GAN design and generated image quality.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gAN architectures and latent interpolation clarifies dC-GAN, StyleGAN, and smooth latent interpolation illustrate practical GAN design and generated image quality, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: GAN versus VAE tradeoffs
kp_catalog.description: GANs can produce crisp samples but lack encoders and are unstable, while VAEs encode x to z but often blur outputs.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gAN versus VAE tradeoffs clarifies gANs can produce crisp samples but lack encoders and are unstable, while VAEs encode x to z but often blur outputs, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
